questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis or less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis.
questions.scenario: The client reports recurrent episodes of severe headache and pounding palpitations.

options.text: More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis
Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis

questions.rationale: Pheochromocytoma crisis is solved faster when the learner bundles headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe hypertension as a catecholamine surge pattern. Chronic, non-episodic findings push the answer away from this diagnosis.

distractorRationales.a: More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis misses the clinical pattern: Pheochromocytoma Crisis hypertension points to the row-specific answer pattern, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.b: Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis misses the clinical pattern: Pheochromocytoma Crisis hypertension points to the row-specific answer pattern, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe episodic hypertension belong together as a pheochromocytoma pattern.
questions.speedCue: The speed clue is episodic sympathetic surge, not slow chronic symptom drift.

questions.stem: Which findings in this febrile client support the nurse's concern for meningococcal sepsis? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A college student arrives with fever, severe headache, malaise, and rapidly worsening illness.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding which additional findings would strengthen concern for a meningococcal emergency rather than a routine viral illness.

options.text: A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash
Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion
Neck stiffness and photophobia
A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure
Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids
Acute confusion or worsening mental status

questions.rationale: Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation, and mental-status change. The nurse should exclude benign or improving patterns that do not fit invasive sepsis.

distractorRationales.d: A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure misses the clinical pattern: Meningococcal Sepsis Petechiae perfusion points to A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash plus Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion plus Neck stiffness and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.e: Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids misses the clinical pattern: Meningococcal Sepsis Petechiae perfusion points to A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash plus Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion plus Neck stiffness and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: The fast clue is not fever alone, but fever plus rash and collapse signs.
questions.speedCue: Nonblanching rash plus neuro change or shock is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move with this back-pain presentation?
questions.scenario: A client with severe low-back pain reports new urinary retention, numbness in the inner thighs, and increasing leg weakness.

Routine lumbar strain that should improve with activity
Expected opioid side effects causing harmless bladder hesitancy
An anxiety response to chronic back pain without neurologic danger

distractorRationales.b: Routine lumbar strain that should improve with activity misses the clinical pattern: Cauda Equina Bladder Retention pain points to Cauda equina syndrome requiring urgent spinal decompression evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: Expected opioid side effects causing harmless bladder hesitancy misses the clinical pattern: Cauda Equina Bladder Retention pain points to Cauda equina syndrome requiring urgent spinal decompression evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: An anxiety response to chronic back pain without neurologic danger misses the clinical pattern: Cauda Equina Bladder Retention pain points to Cauda equina syndrome requiring urgent spinal decompression evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains this client's sudden change after fracture repair?
questions.scenario: A client with a femur fracture becomes acutely confused and hypoxic 24 hours after injury, and a faint petechial rash appears on the chest.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether to frame this as anxiety, pneumonia, or a fracture-related emergency.

options.text: Fat embolism syndrome requiring urgent oxygenation-focused escalation

Expected pain-related tachypnea after fracture stabilization
Minor skin irritation causing an unrelated rash

distractorRationales.d: Minor skin irritation causing an unrelated rash misses the clinical pattern: Fat Embolism Long Bone Fracture hypoxia points to Fat embolism syndrome requiring urgent oxygenation-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move with this endocrine collapse pattern?

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this hemodynamic decline?

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move in this infarction-related hypotension?
questions.scenario: A client with an inferior STEMI becomes more hypotensive after receiving nitroglycerin.

Expected medication relaxation without perfusion significance
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's next move in this oncology emergency?

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's response to this fetal tracing pattern?
questions.scenario: A laboring client receiving oxytocin develops recurrent late fetal heart rate decelerations.
questions.additionalInfo: The contraction pattern is frequent and strong, and the client is lying supine.

options.text: Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption
A reassuring tracing that only needs continued observation
Normal fetal sleep variability without intervention need
A benign maternal discomfort pattern unrelated to the fetus

questions.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with strong oxytocin-driven contractions create a uteroplacental perfusion rescue map. The nurse should answer from intrauterine resuscitation, not from passive monitoring.

distractorRationales.b: A reassuring tracing that only needs continued observation misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue perfusion points to Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Normal fetal sleep variability without intervention need misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue perfusion points to Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: A benign maternal discomfort pattern unrelated to the fetus misses the clinical pattern: Late Deceleration Rescue perfusion points to Uteroplacental perfusion compromise requiring intrauterine resuscitation and oxytocin interruption, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Late decelerations are about placental perfusion, not just strip interpretation.
questions.speedCue: Oxytocin plus recurrent lates is the shortcut to resuscitation actions.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's next move for this postpartum client?
questions.scenario: A postpartum client receiving magnesium sulfate for severe preeclampsia becomes very drowsy.
questions.additionalInfo: Respirations are 8/min, deep tendon reflexes are absent, and urine output has fallen to 18 mL/hr.

options.text: Magnesium toxicity requiring infusion stop, airway support, and antidote-focused escalation
Expected postpartum fatigue that only needs rest
Simple anxiety because the client is sleepy after delivery
A harmless medication effect that can wait for routine reassessment

questions.rationale: Low respirations, absent reflexes, and poor urine output create a magnesium toxicity map. The nurse should answer from airway danger and medication accumulation, not from routine postpartum fatigue.

distractorRationales.b: Expected postpartum fatigue that only needs rest misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity airway points to Magnesium toxicity requiring infusion stop, airway support, and antidote-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Simple anxiety because the client is sleepy after delivery misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity airway points to Magnesium toxicity requiring infusion stop, airway support, and antidote-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: A harmless medication effect that can wait for routine reassessment misses the clinical pattern: Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity airway points to Magnesium toxicity requiring infusion stop, airway support, and antidote-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: The reflex-respiration-urine trio is the shortcut for magnesium toxicity.
questions.speedCue: Absent reflexes plus low respirations should end the debate immediately.

questions.stem: Which clinical map best explains this client's immediate post-anesthesia change?
questions.scenario: Shortly after surgery, a client develops muscle rigidity, rapidly rising end-tidal CO2, tachycardia, and a temperature that is climbing.
questions.additionalInfo: The team is debating whether the fever is the main clue or a later clue.

options.text: Malignant hyperthermia requiring trigger removal, dantrolene, cooling, and aggressive support
Routine postoperative shivering that only needs blankets
Expected anxiety from emergence from anesthesia
Simple dehydration without a true anesthetic emergency

questions.rationale: The fast clue is rigidity with rising end-tidal CO2 after anesthesia. The nurse should answer from malignant hyperthermia rescue rather than waiting for temperature to become the only obvious signal.

distractorRationales.b: Routine postoperative shivering that only needs blankets misses the clinical pattern: Malignant Hyperthermia Initial tachycardia points to Malignant hyperthermia requiring trigger removal, dantrolene, cooling, and aggressive support, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Expected anxiety from emergence from anesthesia misses the clinical pattern: Malignant Hyperthermia Initial tachycardia points to Malignant hyperthermia requiring trigger removal, dantrolene, cooling, and aggressive support, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Simple dehydration without a true anesthetic emergency misses the clinical pattern: Malignant Hyperthermia Initial tachycardia points to Malignant hyperthermia requiring trigger removal, dantrolene, cooling, and aggressive support, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: End-tidal CO2 and rigidity usually beat fever to the diagnosis.
questions.speedCue: CO2 rise plus rigidity after anesthesia is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which clinical map should guide the nurse's next move after this postoperative medication change?
questions.scenario: A postoperative client who received IV morphine 20 minutes ago is difficult to arouse.
questions.additionalInfo: Respirations are 7/min, the oxygen saturation is falling, and the pupils are pinpoint.

options.text: Opioid oversedation requiring airway support and opioid reversal planning
Expected pain relief with deep normal sleep
Routine fatigue from surgery that needs no bedside change
Anxiety because the client is breathing slowly while resting

questions.rationale: Slow respirations, poor arousal, and pinpoint pupils create an opioid-oversedation map. The nurse should answer from ventilation rescue first, not from comfort or observation.

distractorRationales.b: Expected pain relief with deep normal sleep misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation ventilation points to Opioid oversedation requiring airway support and opioid reversal planning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Routine fatigue from surgery that needs no bedside change misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation ventilation points to Opioid oversedation requiring airway support and opioid reversal planning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Anxiety because the client is breathing slowly while resting misses the clinical pattern: Opioid Oversedation ventilation points to Opioid oversedation requiring airway support and opioid reversal planning, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Respiratory depression is the anchor clue, not the pain history.
questions.speedCue: Low respirations plus pinpoint pupils is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority in this postpartum change?
questions.scenario: Three days after delivery, a client reports a severe headache, blurred vision, and epigastric discomfort.
questions.additionalInfo: Blood pressure is 172/108 mm Hg, reflexes are brisk, and the family thinks it may just be exhaustion from the newborn.

options.text: Postpartum preeclampsia with seizure risk requiring urgent antihypertensive and magnesium-focused escalation
Expected postpartum fatigue and dehydration without true danger
A benign tension headache that only needs a dark room
Routine incisional pain presenting in an unusual way

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension, visual change, and hyperreflexia after delivery create a postpartum-preeclampsia map. The nurse should answer from seizure prevention and maternal stabilization, not from fatigue or headache comfort framing.

distractorRationales.b: Expected postpartum fatigue and dehydration without true danger misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Preeclampsia Headache hypertension points to Postpartum preeclampsia with seizure risk requiring urgent antihypertensive and magnesium-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: A benign tension headache that only needs a dark room misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Preeclampsia Headache hypertension points to Postpartum preeclampsia with seizure risk requiring urgent antihypertensive and magnesium-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Routine incisional pain presenting in an unusual way misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Preeclampsia Headache hypertension points to Postpartum preeclampsia with seizure risk requiring urgent antihypertensive and magnesium-focused escalation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Postpartum does not make severe preeclampsia clues less urgent.
questions.speedCue: Headache plus vision change plus severe pressure after delivery is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which decision map should guide the nurse's next move for this client with sickle cell disease?
questions.scenario: A client admitted for a pain crisis now reports chest pain and increasing shortness of breath.
questions.additionalInfo: Temperature is 101.6 F (38.7 C), oxygen saturation has fallen to 88%, and breath sounds are diminished at the bases.

options.text: Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment
A routine pain-crisis variation without pulmonary danger
Expected opioid sedation causing harmless shallow breathing
Simple anxiety because chest pain is common during crises

questions.rationale: Fever, chest pain, and falling oxygenation during a sickle-cell admission create an acute-chest map. The nurse should answer from pulmonary risk, not from generic pain-crisis assumptions.

distractorRationales.b: A routine pain-crisis variation without pulmonary danger misses the clinical pattern: Sickle Cell Acute Chest oxygen points to Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Expected opioid sedation causing harmless shallow breathing misses the clinical pattern: Sickle Cell Acute Chest oxygen points to Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Simple anxiety because chest pain is common during crises misses the clinical pattern: Sickle Cell Acute Chest oxygen points to Acute chest syndrome requiring oxygenation-focused escalation and urgent treatment, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Once oxygenation falls, the question is no longer just about pain.
questions.speedCue: Sickle cell plus chest symptoms plus hypoxia is the shortcut.
